questions.question: Explain the wc command with its commonly used options and give examples.
keyPoints: WC stands for Word Count
Used to count lines, words, bytes in files
-l option counts lines
-w option counts words
-c option counts bytes
-m option counts characters
Can combine options like wc -l -w file.txt
Shows all counts by default: lines words bytes filename

questions.question: What is the od command? Explain its purpose and any four important options with examples.
keyPoints: OD stands for Octal Dump
Used to display file contents in various formats
Essential for examining binary files
-A specifies address base (d/o/x/n)
-t specifies output type (c/d/o/x/f)
-j skips specified bytes
-N limits bytes to display
-v prevents duplicate line suppression

questions.question: How would you use wc command to find: (a) Number of lines in a file (b) Number of words in a file (c) Both lines and words together?
keyPoints: wc -l filename - for line count
wc -w filename - for word count
wc -l -w filename or wc -lw filename - for both
Using < input redirection shows only count without filename
Examples: wc -l report.txt, wc -w notes.txt, wc -lw data.txt

questions.question: Explain how od command can be used to examine binary files. Give an example showing hexadecimal output.
keyPoints: od command is essential for binary file analysis
-t x1 displays hex bytes (1 byte per value)
-A n removes address column
-v shows all lines (no duplication)
Example: od -A n -t x1 -v binaryfile.bin
Shows raw byte values in readable format
Useful for debugging file formats

questions.question: Differentiate between wc and od commands in Linux.
keyPoints: WC = Word Count, OD = Octal Dump
WC counts statistics, OD displays content
WC works on text files primarily
OD works on any file including binary
WC output is numeric counts
OD output shows actual byte values
WC is for file statistics
OD is for file inspection and debugging

questions.question: What will be the output of the following commands? (a) wc -l file.txt (b) wc -c file.txt (c) cat file.txt | wc -w
keyPoints: wc -l displays number of lines in file.txt
wc -c displays number of bytes in file.txt
cat file.txt | wc -w displays word count
When using pipe, output shows only count without filename
Default wc output includes filename when file is specified
Using redirection (<) or pipe (|) suppresses filename in output

questions.question: Explain the significance of od command in system administration and debugging.
keyPoints: OD reveals hidden/non-printable characters
Essential for analyzing binary file formats
Helps in debugging file corruption issues
Can examine raw disk sectors and device files
Useful for understanding executable file headers
Aids in recovering data from damaged files
Can inspect network packet dumps
Essential for low-level system programming

questions.question: Explain the od command in detail with emphasis on its various formatting options. Provide examples showing how to display file content in ASCII, hexadecimal, and decimal formats.
keyPoints: OD command overview and purpose
-A option for address base control with examples
-t option for type specification
Displaying ASCII: od -t c
Displaying Hex: od -t x1 or od -t x2
Displaying Decimal: od -t d1
Combining options: od -A n -t x1 -v
Practical examples for each format
Understanding the output format
Common use cases for each format

questions.question: Write a detailed note on the wc command explaining all its options. Also write a shell script that uses wc to count total lines, words, and characters in multiple files and display the results in a formatted manner.
keyPoints: WC command introduction and syntax
All options: -l, -w, -c, -m, -L
Default behavior and output format
Using multiple options together
Script to process multiple files
Using wc in loops or with find command
Formatting output in script
Handling edge cases
Practical applications in automation

questions.question: Describe practical applications of wc and od commands in a Linux system administrator's daily tasks. Include examples of how these commands can be used for file analysis and system monitoring.
keyPoints: WC for log file analysis - counting error entries
WC for code metrics - lines of code counting
WC for file verification and comparison
OD for examining binary logs and core dumps
OD for analyzing file headers and magic numbers
OD for inspecting device files and raw data
Combined use in shell scripts for reporting
Real-world admin scenarios
Security and forensic applications

questions.question: Explain with examples how to use od command to: (a) Skip first N bytes of a file (b) Display only specific number of bytes (c) Display file content without address column (d) Show duplicate lines in output.
keyPoints: -j option for skipping bytes: od -j 512 file.bin
-N option for limiting bytes: od -N 100 file.bin
-A n to remove address column: od -A n -t c file
-v to show all lines: od -v file.bin
Combined example showing all options
Understanding output interpretation
Practical scenarios for each use case
